article:
Chunk # 0.
Chapter 9
Isolation of Physiologically Active and Intact Mitochondria
from Chickpea
Sonika Pandey, Aprajita Kumari, and Kapuganti Jagadis Gupta
Abstract
Chickpea is an important leguminous crop that belongs to Fabaceae family, highly valued for its nutritious
seeds Seeds contain reserve food for the developing embryos Mitochondria are crucial organelle for
generation of chemical energy in the form of ATP which is required for achieving metabolically active
state; therefore, investigating mitochondrial function and respiration rate is crucial for exploring various
metabolic and physio-biochemical changes that occur during seed germination Here we describe a method
for isolation of mitochondria from germinating seeds of two chickpea varieties, i.e., Desi and Kabuli Structure of Mitotracker-stained isolated mitochondria was observed by confocal microscopy and respira-
tion rate was measured using an oxygen microsensor Key words Chickpea, Confocal, Germination, Mitochondria, Oxygen sensor, Respiration rate
1 Introduction
Chickpea ( Cicer arietinum L.) belongs to Fabaceae family and is
the third most important pulse, cultivated in semiarid regions of
Asia and Africa Among all countries, India is the world leader in
chickpea production, covering approximately 8.3 million hectares
of land that accounts for 0.33 million tons of seed production [ 1] Chickpea gets nitrogen via association with rhizobacteria [ 2] Seeds
of Chickpea are consumed in diets for their protein, carbohydrate,
and essential amino acid contents [ 3] In addition, these seeds are
also rich source of minerals (calcium, phosphorous, iron, and zinc),
β-carotene, and crude ﬁbers [ 4] Based on phenotypic appearances
and geographical distribution chickpea is characterized into two
groups: Desi and Kabuli Desi variety grows as small bush bearing
small leaﬂets and small dark-colored angular seeds [ 5] However,
Kabuli plant is grown mainly in the Mediterranean region, and
forms white ﬂowers that later produce large, yellowish colored
oval seeds Kapuganti Jagadis Gupta (ed.), Plant Respiration and Internal Oxygen: Methods and Protocols , Methods in Molecular Biology,
vol 1670, DOI 10.1007/978-1-4939-7292-0_9, ©Springer Science+Business Media LLC 2017
77During seed germination, rapid mobilization of storage
reserves occurs that initiates growth and morphological alterations
and also results in changes in physiological, biochemical, and meta-
bolic activity [ 6] Seed germination is totally an energy-dependent
phenomenon mediated by generation of ATP by mitochondria Plant mitochondria is a complex double-membrane organelle
and is associated with biosynthesis (ATP/ADP) via generation of
proton motive force [ 7] Out of many factors oxygen is an essential
component for mitochondria to produce ATP and speed germina-
tion associated with increased respiration rate, which is accompa-
nied by the mitochondrial biogenesis [ 8] Therefore studying
mitochondrial function using germinating seed as a model system
can be useful for exploring the mitochondrial biogenesis and
energy metabolism during seed germination process In this
work, we have described a protocol for isolating metabolically
active mitochondria from mature germinating seeds of two differ-
ent chickpea varieties, i.e., Desi and Kabuli The isolated mitochon-
dria were further used for confocal imaging and studying the
change in respiration rate using an oxygen microsensor 2 Materials
2.1 Plant Material Germinated chickpea seeds 50 g ( seeNote 1 ) 2.2 Reagents 1.Buffers : Prepare the following buffers 1 day before (a)Extraction buffer : Prepare 500 ml buffer for extraction of
mitochondria by dissolving different components in
double-distilled water [HEPES (100 mM, pH 7.2), sucrose
(0.3 M), skim milk (0.1%), polyvinylpyrrolidone (0.6%),
EDTA (1 mM), L-cysteine (4 mM), KH 2PO4(5 mM),
and MgCl 2(2 mM)] Store buffer at 4/C14C(seeNote 2 ) (b) Suspension buffer : Prepare 100 ml suspension buffer that
comprises various components dissolved in double-
distilled water [HEPES (20 mM, pH 7.2), sucrose
(0.3 M), EDTA (1 mM), MgCl 2(2 mM), KH 2PO4
(0.5 mM)] Store buffer at 4/C14C(seeNote 2 ) 2.Percoll gradient : Prepare by mixing varying percentage of Per-
coll (60, 45, 28, and 5%) with suspension buffer of ﬁnal volume
20 ml Discontinuous Percoll gradient should be prepared
freshly into 50 ml Oakridge tube, starting from bottom to
top: 6 ml of 60%, 8 ml of each 45%, 28% and 5%, respectively,
using a 10 ml syringe (Fig 1i)(seeNote 3 ) Prepared discon-
tinuous Percoll gradient must be chilled on ice before using 3.Bradford reagent  4.MitoTracker Red : Dissolve lyophilized powder in high-quality
DMSO Store the solution in the dark at /C020/C14C.78 Sonika Pandey et al.5.Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NADH) : Prepare 0.5 mM
NADH by dissolving the powder in Milli-Q water and store in
aliquots at /C020/C14C 2.3 Equipment Centrifuge tubes 1 l, 50 ml, and 1.5 ml Plastic beakers Scissors Electric grinder Spatula Measuring cylinder Nitrile gloves Tissue paper Sandwich of miracloth and surgical cotton Syringe (10 ml) Cut tips (0.2 and 1 ml) Micropipettes Magnetic pellet Magnetic stirrer Marker Ice ﬂakes Camera Centrifuge machine (Sorvall, Lynx 6000) MitoTracker Red (Molecular Probes, Life Technologies, USA) Fig 1 Illustration of mitochondria isolated from geminated seeds of chickpea

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. Prepare extraction buffer by dissolving different components in double-distilled water: HEPES (100 mM, pH 7.2), sucrose (0.3 M), skim milk (0.1%), polyvinylpyrrolidone (0.6%), EDTA (1 mM), L-cysteine (4 mM), KH2PO4 (5 mM), and MgCl2 (2 mM). Store buffer at 4°C.

2. Prepare suspension buffer by dissolving various components in double-distilled water: HEPES (20 mM, pH 7.2), sucrose (0.3 M), EDTA (1 mM), MgCl2 (2 mM), KH2PO4 (0.5 mM). Store buffer at 4°C.

3. Prepare a Percoll gradient by mixing varying percentages of Percoll (60, 45, 28, and 5%) with suspension buffer to make a final volume of 20 ml. Prepare the discontinuous Percoll gradient freshly in a 50 ml Oakridge tube, starting from the bottom to the top: 6 ml of 60%, 8 ml of each 45%, 28%, and 5%, respectively.

4. Dissolve MitoTracker Red lyophilized powder in high-quality DMSO. Store the solution in the dark at -20°C.

5. Prepare 0.5 mM NADH by dissolving the powder in Milli-Q water and store in aliquots at -20°C.

6. Use germinated chickpea seeds as the plant material.

7. Perform the isolation process using the following equipment: centrifuge tubes (1 L, 50 ml, and 1.5 ml), plastic beakers, scissors, electric grinder, spatula, measuring cylinder, nitrile gloves, tissue paper, sandwich of miracloth and surgical cotton, syringe (10 ml) with cut tips (0.2 and 1 ml), micropipettes, magnetic pellet, magnetic stirrer, marker, ice flakes, camera, and a centrifuge machine (Sorvall, Lynx 6000).

**Incompleteness or Uncertainties:**

- The article does not provide specific details on the isolation process itself, such as the steps involved in extracting mitochondria from the germinated chickpea seeds.
- The article does not mention the specific duration or conditions for the isolation process.
- The article does not mention the specific steps or equipment used for confocal imaging or studying the change in respiration rate using an oxygen microsensor.

**Conclusion:**

The article provides the necessary information for preparing the extraction buffer, suspension buffer, and Percoll gradient for isolating mitochondria from germinated chickpea seeds. However, it lacks details on the actual isolation process and the steps involved. Additionally, the article does not mention the steps or equipment used for confocal imaging or studying the change in respiration rate. Further information is needed to complete the mitochondrial isolation protocol.

Organisms Mentioned in the Mitochondrial Extraction Protocol:
- Germinated chickpea seeds

summary:
Summary 5:
Sonika Pandey, Aprajita Kumari, and Kapuganti Jagadis Gupta conducted research on the isolation of physiologically active and intact mitochondria from germinating chickpea seeds. Chickpea, a leguminous crop belonging to the Fabaceae family, is cultivated in semiarid regions of Asia and Africa. It is valued for its nutritious seeds, which are rich in protein, carbohydrates, amino acids, minerals (calcium, phosphorous, iron, zinc), β-carotene, and crude fibers. Chickpea production is prominent in India, covering approximately 8.3 million hectares of land. Nitrogen fixation in chickpea occurs through association with rhizobacteria. The researchers isolated mitochondria from germinating seeds of two chickpea varieties, Desi and Kabuli, using a percoll gradient and centrifugation. The isolated mitochondria were visualized using confocal microscopy and their respiration rate was measured using an oxygen microsensor. The findings contribute to understanding the role of mitochondria in chickpea seed germination and energy metabolism. The research was conducted at Molecular Probes, a division of Life Technologies, located in the USA.

3.1 Germination
of Chickpea Seeds1 Take chickpea seeds in a Falcon tube and incubate them in 0.1%
HgCl 2for a period of 10 min for sterilization During steriliza-
tion shake the falcon tube containing seeds After that, rinse

(seeNote 4 ) 3 After imbibition, take the seeds from water and cover and tie it
in wet miracloth 4 Keep the wrapped seeds in dark incubation for germination,
i.e., emergence of radicle (Fig 1a, b) 5 Record the weight of germinated seeds before starting mito-

Isolation of

buffer in cooled electric grinder This step should be repeated
ten times in short spin so that seeds can be homogenized
completely and uniformly Make ﬁnal volume up to 200 ml

wiched between double-layered miracloth to separate liquid

bottle (Fig 1d) 4 Centrifuge the ﬁltrate at low spin at 500 /C2gfor 10 min at 4/C14C Transfer the supernatant into fresh centrifuge bottle (1 l)
carefully 5 Again centrifuge the supernatant at 2000 /C2gfor 15 min and

bottom contains mitochondrial fractions along with other cel-
lular impurities 7 Dissolve the pellet in 2–4 ml suspension buffer and load it
carefully into prechilled Percoll gradient prepared in centrifuge
tube (50 ml) (Fig 1i) Centrifuge at 17,000 /C2gfor an hour at
4/C14C 8 Mitochondrial fraction can be seen dispersed as whitish creamy
layer in zone of 45 and 28% Percoll gradient (Fig 1j) Carefully
collect the mitochondrial fraction using 1 ml cut tip and trans-
fer into 50 ml centrifuge tube and add 5 ml suspension buffer 9 Again centrifuge at 17,000 /C2gfor 10 min at 4/C14C This step is
required to remove the content of Percoll gradient Discard the
supernatant and gently dissolve the pellet in 1 ml suspension
buffer 10 Prepare the aliquots in 1.5 ml Eppendorf tube, freeze in liquid
nitrogen, and store at /C020/C14C for further experiments 11 Stepwise illustration (Fig 1a–j) and schematic representation

chickpea are shown 3.3 Estimation of
Mitochondrial Protein
Concentration1 First prepare stock of bovine albumin serum (BSA) protein
0.1μg/μl in double-distilled water 2 Prepare a standard curve of BSA protein 0–10 μg using Brad-
ford reagent and record the absorbance at 595 nm 3 Take 10 μl of unknown protein sample and mix well with 990 μl

obtained from BSA standard curve 3.4 Imaging
Mitochondria in
Confocal Microscopy1 Mix gently 50 μl of freshly isolated mitochondria with suspen-
sion buffer 2 Add 500 nM MitoTracker Red dye into mitochondrial suspen-
sion and keep it in dark incubation for 10 min at room
temperature.Isolation of Physiologically Active and Intact Mitochondria from Chickpea 813 For imaging, add 3 μl of mitochondrial suspension onto a glass
slide and gently place coverslip over it 4 Choose the appropriate excitation (488 nm) and emission
(500–530 nm) wavelengths for MitoTracker Red dye 5 Adjust laser intensity of the images on a Zeiss imaging ﬂuores-
cence microscope (60 /C2/1.0 numeric aperture [NA] oil objec-
tive) equipped with a Leica TCS SP5 X (Leica Microsystems)
inverted confocal laser scanning microscope and capture the
image (Fig 3) 6 The acquisition software is LAS AF software (Leica
Microsystems)

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for Isolation of Mitochondria from Germinated Chickpea Seeds:

1. Germination of Chickpea Seeds:
   a. Take chickpea seeds in a Falcon tube and incubate them in 0.1% HgCl2 for 10 min for sterilization.
   b. Shake the Falcon tube containing seeds during sterilization.
   c. Rinse seeds 3-4 times with autoclaved water to remove HgCl2 completely.
   d. Soak the sterilized seeds in sterile water for 4 hours for imbibition.
   e. After imbibition, take the seeds from water and cover and tie them in wet miracloth.
   f. Keep the wrapped seeds in a dark incubation for germination until the radicle emerges.
   g. Record the weight of germinated seeds before starting mitochondrial isolation.

2. Steps for Isolation of Mitochondria:
   a. Take germinated seeds and rinse them in distilled water.
   b. Homogenize 50 g of seeds in 50 ml of prechilled extraction buffer in a cooled electric grinder. Repeat this step ten times in short spins to ensure complete and uniform homogenization. Make the final volume up to 200 ml.
   c. Squeeze the homogenate through a single layer of cotton sandwiched between double-layered miracloth to separate the liquid part from the solid tissue. Collect the filtrate in a 1 ml centrifuge bottle.
   d. Centrifuge the filtrate at low spin at 500 × g for 10 min at 4°C. Transfer the supernatant into a fresh centrifuge bottle carefully.
   e. Again centrifuge the supernatant at 2000 × g for 15 min and collect the supernatant.
   f. Centrifuge the supernatant at 12,000 × g for an hour at 4°C. Discard the supernatant slowly as the pellet formed at the bottom contains mitochondrial fractions along with other cellular impurities.
   g. Dissolve the pellet in 2-4 ml suspension buffer and load it carefully into a prechilled Percoll gradient prepared in a centrifuge tube (50 ml). Centrifuge at 17,000 × g for an hour at 4°C.
   h. The mitochondrial fraction can be seen dispersed as a whitish creamy layer in the zone of 45 and 28% Percoll gradient. Carefully collect the mitochondrial fraction using a 1 ml cut tip and transfer it into a 50 ml centrifuge tube. Add 5 ml suspension buffer.
   i. Again centrifuge at 17,000 × g for 10 min at 4°C to remove the content of the Percoll gradient. Discard the supernatant and gently dissolve the pellet in 1 ml suspension buffer.
   j. Prepare aliquots in 1.5 ml Eppendorf tubes, freeze them in liquid nitrogen, and store at -20°C for further experiments.

Conclusion:
The article provides a detailed protocol for the isolation of mitochondria from germinated chickpea seeds. The steps mentioned cover the process from seed germination to the collection of the mitochondrial fraction. However, there are some gaps in the protocol, such as the specific composition of the extraction buffer and the suspension buffer. Additionally, the article does not provide details on the balancing of the extract and the preparation of the Percoll gradient. These missing details should be addressed to ensure complete reproducibility of the protocol.
mitochondria.organisms:
Organisms Mentioned in the Mitochondrial Extraction Protocol:
- Germinated Chickpea Seeds (Cicer arietinum)

summary: Summary 7: Germinated seeds of Desi and Kabuli are homogenized to obtain an extract, which is then centrifuged to isolate mitochondria. The resulting pellet shows debris, while the supernatant contains impurities and cellular debris. The isolation procedure involves sterilization, imbibition, dark incubation, and weight measurement. Various equipment and materials, including centrifuges, glass slides, coverslips, oxygen microsensors, and a confocal microscope, are used. The homogenization process is repeated multiple times to ensure complete and uniform homogenization. A prechilled Percoll gradient is prepared to further isolate the mitochondrial fractions. Physiologically active and intact mitochondria are obtained from chickpea seeds. The protein concentration is estimated using the Bradford reagent and a standard curve. The mitochondria are imaged using MitoTracker Red dye and a confocal microscope. Figures are included to illustrate the process and results.

It also mentions the various equipment and materials used, such as centrifuges, glass slides, coverslips, oxygen microsensors, and a confocal microscope.

of oxygen sensor as mentioned below:

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for Mitochondrial Isolation from Germinated Seeds of Chickpea:

1. Centrifuge filtered extract at 500 ×g for 10 min at 4 °C and transfer supernatant into fresh tube.
2. Centrifuge the transferred supernatant at 2,000 ×g for 15 min.
3. Collect the supernatant and centrifuge it at 12,000 ×g for an hour.
4. The resulting pellet contains the mitochondrial fraction along with other cellular impurities.
5. The mitochondrial fraction can be further purified using a discontinuous Percoll gradient.
6. Collect the mitochondrial fraction, which lies between 45% and 28% Percoll gradient, using a 1 ml cut tip and transfer it into a 50 ml Oakridge tube.
7. Centrifuge the Oakridge tube at 17,000 ×g for 10 min at 4 °C.
8. Dissolve the pellet in 1 ml suspension buffer and store it at 4 °C.
9. Use the isolated mitochondria for confocal imaging and respiration measurement.

Steps for Measurement of Mitochondrial Respiration of Seed:

1. Take isolated mitochondria equivalent to 1 mg protein and make the final volume 1 ml with suspension buffer.
2. Put a magnetic pellet inside an HPLC vial and seal it with parafilm.
3. Keep the vial on a magnetic stirrer for 5 min.
4. Pierce the cap of the HPLC vial at the top using a needle and flush with ambient air for 5 min.
5. Remove the needle and carefully insert the oxygen sensor into the vial.
6. Record the change in respiration at 1 min intervals.
7. Add 0.5 mM NADH and record the rate of consumption at every 1-s interval.

Notes:
- The article does not provide complete details for the calibration of the oxygen sensor.
- The article mentions the use of a suspension buffer but does not provide the composition or preparation method.
- The article mentions the use of an extraction buffer but does not provide the composition or preparation method.

Conclusion:
The article provides a detailed protocol for the isolation of mitochondria from germinated seeds of chickpea. However, there are some gaps in the protocol, such as the lack of details for the calibration of the oxygen sensor and the composition and preparation methods for the suspension buffer and extraction buffer. These missing details could affect the reproducibility of the protocol.

summary:
Summary: 
- Respiration is measured using the TBR1025 single-channel free radical analyzer.
- The oxygen sensor is calibrated by washing it with distilled water, flushing nitrogen gas, and setting zero level oxygen using software.
- Mitochondrial fraction is obtained by centrifuging and purifying the pellet using a discontinuous Percoll gradient.
- Isolated mitochondria are used for confocal imaging and respiration measurement.
- The process of isolating physiologically active and intact mitochondria from chickpea seeds involves various steps and precautions.
- World Precision Instruments in Sarasota, FL, USA, is mentioned in Figure 4 of the paper by Pandey et al, Chapter 6.
- Other entities mentioned include HPLC vial, Nitrogen gas, Air pump, Suspension buffer, Germination, Centrifuge, Oakridge tube, Electric grinder, Extraction buffer, Chickpea, Paraﬁlm, Magnetic stirrer, Needle, Figure 5, NIPGR, IYBA, Ramalingaswami, Department of Biotechnology, FAO, Carranca C, De Varennes A, Rolston D, Eur J Agron, Jukanti AK, Gaur PM, Gowda CLL, Chibbar RN, Br J Nutr, Pradhan S, Bandhiwal N, Shah N, Kant C, Gaur R, Bhatia S, Desi Kabuli, Time(s), Mededlingen Landbouw Hogeschool, Martı ´n JJ, Tocino A´, Ardanuy R, Juana G, Cervantes E, Acta Physiol Plant, Bowsher CG, Tobin AK, J Exp Bot, Oxygen Sensor, Confocal imaging, Calibration, Zero level of oxygen, NADH, Oxidation rate, Oxygen microsensor, Buffers, Percoll gradient, Healthy seeds, Homogenization, Schematic representation, Magnetic pellet, Consumption rate, Freshly prepared, Ice chilled, Breakage, Acknowledgements.

- Nitrogen Gas: Flush nitrogen gas into HPLC vial for 5 min

- Filtration: Centrifuge filtered extract at 500 ×g for 10 min at 4 °C
- Mitochondrial Fraction: Pellet contains mitochondrial fraction along with other cellular impurities
- Percoll Gradient: Can be purified using discontinuous Percoll gradient
- Oakridge Tube: Collect mitochondrial fraction using 1 ml cut tip
- Respiration Measurement: Use isolated mitochondria for respiration measurement
- Pellet: Dissolve pellet in 2-4 ml of suspension buffer and load into Percoll gradient
- Isolation: Isolation of Physiologically Active and Intact Mitochondria from Chickpea
- Extraction Buffer: Homogenize seeds in electric grinder along with extraction buffer at 4 °C
- Weight: Record weight of germinated seeds
- Homogenize: Homogenize seeds in electric grinder along with extraction buffer at 4 °C
- Magnetic Pellet: Put magnetic pellet inside HPLC vial and seal it with parafilm
- Magnetic Stirrer: Keep it on magnetic stirrer for 5 min
- Consumption Rate: Record the change at 1 min interval
- Freshly Prepared: All buffers must be prepared freshly and should be ice chilled before using
- Ice Chilled: All buffers must be prepared freshly and should be ice chilled before using
- Breakage: Oxygen microsensor must be carefully inserted into the HPLC vial to prevent breakage

mitochondria.protocol:
**Article:** Logan DC, Millar AH, Sweetlove LJ, Hill SA, Leaver CJ (2001) Mitochondrial biogenesis during germination in maize embryos Plant Physiol 125:662–672

**Section:** Isolation of Physiologically Active and Intact Mitochondria from Chickpea

**Steps:**
1. "Mitochondria were isolated from 50 g of wet weight of chickpea (Cicer arietinum) cotyledons by differential centrifugation."
2. "The tissue was homogenized in 250 mL of ice-cold isolation medium containing 0.3 M sucrose, 10 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.5), 1 mM EDTA, 1 mM EGTA, 0.1% (w/v) BSA, 1% (w/v) PVP, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v) polyvinylpolypyrrolidone, 1% (w/v

topics.topic: Isolation of physiologically active and intact mitochondria from chickpea
